html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Five — Part Three

The Book of Iyov

IYOV

Wisdom — Elihu and the Voice From the Whirlwind

Chapters 29 through 42

I Had Heard of You With My Ears But Now My Eye Sees You — You Have Not Spoken of Me What Is Right as My Servant Iyov Has — YHWH Blessed the Latter Days of Iyov More Than His Beginning

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — 4QpaleoJobc — 2QJob — 11QtgJob Aramaic Targum

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

IYOV — CHAPTERS 29 THROUGH 31 — THE GREAT OATH OF INNOCENCE

IYOV — CHAPTER 29 Oh That I Were as in the Months of Old — When Creator Watched Over Me — When My Steps Were Bathed in Butter — I Was Eyes to the Blind — I Was a Father to the Needy — I Thought I Shall Die in My Nest

1 And Iyov again took up his discourse and said:

2 Oh that I were as in the months of old, as in the days when Creator watched over me,

3 When his lamp shone upon my head, and by his light I walked through darkness,

4 As I was in my prime, when the friendship of Creator was upon my tent,

5 When the Almighty was yet with me, when my children were all around me,

6 When my steps were washed with butter, and the rock poured out for me streams of oil.

7 When I went out to the gate of the city, when I prepared my seat in the square,

8 The young men saw me and withdrew, and the aged rose and stood.

9 The princes refrained from talking and laid their hand on their mouth.

10 The voice of the nobles was hushed, and their tongue stuck to the roof of their mouth.

11 When the ear heard, it called me blessed, and when the eye saw, it approved,

12 Because I delivered the poor who cried for help, and the fatherless who had none to help him.

13 The blessing of him who was about to perish came upon me, and I caused the widow's heart to sing for joy.

14 I put on righteousness, and it clothed me. My justice was like a robe and a turban.

15 I was eyes to the blind and feet to the lame.

16 I was a father to the needy, and I searched out the cause of him I did not know.

17 I broke the fangs of the unrighteous and made him drop his prey from his teeth.

18 Then I thought, I shall die in my nest, and I shall multiply my days as the sand,

19 My roots spread out to the waters, with the dew all night on my branches,

20 My glory fresh with me, and my bow ever new in my hand.

21 Men listened to me and waited and kept silence for my counsel.

22 After I spoke they did not speak again, and my word dropped upon them.

23 They waited for me as for the rain, and they opened their mouths as for the spring rain.

24 I smiled on them when they had no confidence, and the light of my face they did not cast down.

25 I chose their way and sat as chief, and I lived like a king among his troops, like one who comforts mourners.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 29 opens the longest covenant speech in the book — Iyov's final speech spanning Chapters 29-31. Chapter 29 is the covenant retrospective: Iyov describes the covenant past when YHWH's covenant friendship was upon his tent — verse 4 — and when his covenant social covenant position and covenant covenant faithfulness were mutually covenant reinforcing. The covenant past is covenant beautiful precisely because the covenant contrast with the covenant present is so devastating.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 29 in full. Verse 12-17 — Iyov's covenant social covenant biography: he delivered the poor, the fatherless, the widow, the blind, the lame, the needy. He broke the fangs of the unrighteous. The covenant social covenant justice that Elifaz accused Iyov of lacking — Chapter 22:6-9 — is here described by Iyov himself in precise covenant detail. The covenant indictment and the covenant self-defense occupy exactly the same covenant categories.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 29 in full. Verse 14 — I put on righteousness, and it clothed me. My justice was like a robe and a turban. The covenant metaphor of covenant righteousness as covenant clothing is the covenant central covenant self-image of Iyov's covenant past: his covenant character was so thoroughly covenant clothed in covenant righteousness that it was his covenant defining covenant appearance. Elifaz's covenant accusation that Iyov stripped the naked — Chapter 22:6 — is covenant precisely inverted by Iyov's covenant self-description.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — when the friendship of Creator was upon my tent. The covenant word for friendship — sod — is the covenant same word used for the heavenly council in Chapter 15:8 — have you listened in the council of Creator? Iyov's covenant former covenant experience of YHWH was one of covenant intimate covenant friendship — covenant access to the covenant divine covenant counsel. The covenant loss of this covenant friendship is the covenant deepest covenant dimension of Iyov's covenant suffering.

IYOV — CHAPTER 30 But Now They Mock Me — They Are Sons of Fools — Creator Has Cast Me Into the Mire — I Cry to You and You Do Not Answer — Yet Disaster Comes Upon Me — My Inward Parts Are in Tumult

1 But now they laugh at me, men who are younger than I, whose fathers I would have disdained to set with the dogs of my flock.

2 What could I gain from the strength of their hands, men whose vigor is gone?

3 Through want and hard hunger they gnaw the dry ground by night in waste and desolation.

4 They pick saltwort and the leaves of bushes, and the roots of the broom tree for their warmth.

5 They are driven out from human company. They shout after them as after a thief.

6 In the gullies of the torrents they must dwell, in holes of the earth and of the rocks.

7 Among the bushes they bray. Under the nettles they huddle together.

8 A senseless, a nameless brood, they have been whipped out of the land.

9 And now I have become their song. I am a byword to them.

10 They abhor me. They keep aloof from me. They do not hesitate to spit at the sight of me.

11 Because Creator has loosed my cord and humbled me, they have cast off restraint in my presence.

12 On my right hand the rabble rise. They push away my feet. They cast up against me their ways of destruction.

13 They break up my path. They promote my calamity. They need no one to help them.

14 As through a wide breach they come. Amid the crash they roll on.

15 Terrors are turned upon me. My honor is pursued as by the wind, and my prosperity has passed away like a cloud.

16 And now my soul is poured out within me. Days of affliction have taken hold of me.

17 The night racks my bones, and the pain that gnaws me takes no rest.

18 With great force my garment is disfigured. It binds me about like the collar of my tunic.

19 Creator has cast me into the mire, and I have become like dust and ashes.

20 I cry to you for help and you do not answer me. I stand, and you only look at me.

21 You have turned cruel to me. With the might of your hand you persecute me.

22 You lift me up on the wind. You make me ride on it, and you toss me about in the roar of the storm.

23 For I know that you will bring me to death and to the house appointed for all living.

24 Yet does not one in a heap of ruins stretch out his hand, and in his disaster cry for help?

25 Did I not weep for him whose day was hard? Was not my soul grieved for the needy?

26 But when I hoped for good, evil came, and when I waited for light, darkness came.

27 My inward parts are in turmoil and never still. Days of affliction come to meet me.

28 I go about darkened, but not by the sun. I stand up in the assembly and cry for help.

29 I am a brother of jackals and a companion of ostriches.

30 My skin turns black and falls from me, and my bones burn with heat.

31 My lyre is turned to mourning, and my pipe to the voice of those who weep.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 30 is the devastating covenant present — the covenant counterpart to Chapter 29's covenant beautiful past. The covenant contrast is systematic: every covenant positive element of Chapter 29 has a covenant negative counterpart in Chapter 30. Where he was respected by the aged and nobles — Chapter 29:8-9 — he is now mocked by the youngest and most despised — Chapter 30:1. Where YHWH was his covenant friend — Chapter 29:4 — YHWH has now cast him into the mire — Chapter 30:19.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 30 in full. Verse 20 — I cry to you for help and you do not answer me. I stand, and you only look at me. The covenant silence of YHWH in the face of Iyov's covenant cry is the most painful element of the covenant suffering: it is not only the covenant physical covenant pain but the covenant absence of the covenant divine covenant response that constitutes the covenant deepest covenant dimension of covenant suffering.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 30 in full. Verse 26 — but when I hoped for good, evil came, and when I waited for light, darkness came. The covenant structural reversal of Iyov's covenant expectations: the covenant man of covenant integrity whose covenant theology predicted covenant good following covenant obedience now experiences the covenant precise opposite. The covenant gap between the covenant theological prediction and the covenant experiential reality is the covenant center of the covenant book.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25 — did I not weep for him whose day was hard? Was not my soul grieved for the needy? Iyov's covenant contrast between his covenant past covenant compassion for the covenant suffering and the covenant present absence of covenant compassion for him: he wept for those whose day was hard — but when his own day became hard, no covenant weeping came from YHWH or from the covenant community. The covenant empathy Iyov showed is not returned to him in his covenant suffering.

IYOV — CHAPTER 31 I Have Made a Covenant With My Eyes — Let Creator Weigh Me in a Just Balance — If I Have Walked With Falsehood — If My Heart Has Been Enticed by a Woman — If I Have Rejected the Cause of My Servant — If I Have Withheld Bread From the Hungry — Oh That I Had the Indictment Written by My Adversary

1 I have made a covenant with my eyes. How then could I gaze at a virgin?

2 What would be my portion from Creator above and my heritage from the Almighty on high?

3 Is not calamity for the unrighteous, and disaster for workers of iniquity?

4 Does not he see my ways and number all my steps?

5 If I have walked with falsehood and my foot has hastened to deceit,

6 Let me be weighed in a just balance, and let Creator know my integrity.

7 If my step has turned aside from the way and my heart has gone after my eyes, and if any spot has stuck to my hands,

8 Then let me sow, and another eat, and let what grows for me be rooted out.

9 If my heart has been enticed toward a woman, and I have lain in wait at my neighbor's door,

10 Then let my wife grind for another, and let others bow down on her.

11 For that would be a heinous crime. That would be an iniquity to be punished by the judges.

12 For that is a fire that consumes as far as Abaddon, and it would burn to the root all my increase.

13 If I have rejected the cause of my manservant or my maidservant, when they brought a complaint against me,

14 What then shall I do when Creator rises up? When he makes inquiry, what shall I answer him?

15 Did not he who made me in the womb make him? And did not one fashion us in the womb?

16 If I have withheld anything that the poor desired, or have caused the eyes of the widow to fail,

17 Or have eaten my morsel alone, and the fatherless has not eaten of it,

18 For from my youth the fatherless grew up with me as with a father, and from my mother's womb I guided the widow —

19 If I have seen anyone perish for lack of clothing, or the needy without covering,

20 If his body has not blessed me, and if he was not warmed with the fleece of my sheep,

21 If I have raised my hand against the fatherless, because I saw my help in the gate,

22 Then let my shoulder blade fall from my shoulder, and let my arm be broken from its socket.

23 For I was in terror of calamity from Creator, and I could not have faced his majesty.

24 If I have made gold my trust or called fine gold my confidence,

25 If I have rejoiced because my wealth was abundant or because my hand had found much,

26 If I have looked at the sun when it shone, or the moon moving in splendor,

27 And my heart has been secretly enticed, and my mouth has kissed my hand,

28 This also would be an iniquity to be punished by the judges, for I would have been false to Creator above.

29 If I have rejoiced at the ruin of him who hated me, or exulted when evil overtook him

30 — I have not let my mouth sin by asking for his life with a curse —

31 If the men of my tent have not said, Who is there that has not been filled with his meat?

32 The sojourner has not lodged in the street. I have opened my doors to the road —

33 If I have concealed my transgressions as others do by hiding my iniquity in my heart,

34 Because I stood in great fear of the multitude, and the contempt of families terrified me, so that I kept silence, and did not go out of doors —

35 Oh that I had one to hear me. Here is my signature. Let the Almighty answer me. Oh that I had the indictment written by my adversary.

36 Surely I would carry it on my shoulder. I would bind it on me as a crown.

37 I would give him an account of all my steps. Like a prince I would approach him.

38 If my land has cried out against me and its furrows have wept together,

39 If I have eaten its yield without payment and made its owners breathe their last,

40 Let thorns grow instead of wheat, and foul weeds instead of barley. The words of Iyov are ended.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 31 is the covenant greatest covenant oath of covenant innocence in the covenant Hebrew canon: Iyov swears a formal covenant oath covering every category of covenant moral life — sexual covenant integrity, covenant business covenant honesty, covenant treatment of servants, covenant care for the poor and widows, covenant relationship to wealth and idolatry, covenant treatment of enemies, covenant hospitality, covenant self-deception. The covenant oath is the covenant final covenant statement of the covenant man who has held fast his covenant integrity.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 31 in full. Verse 35-37 — oh that I had one to hear me. Here is my signature. Let the Almighty answer me. Oh that I had the indictment written by my adversary. Surely I would carry it on my shoulder. I would bind it on me as a crown. Iyov's covenant final demand: he wants the covenant legal covenant indictment that YHWH would bring against him so that he can respond. The covenant man who has been treated as covenant guilty demands to see the covenant charges.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 31 in full. Verse 40 — the words of Iyov are ended. The covenant editorial statement that marks the close of Iyov's covenant formal covenant case: after the covenant magnificent oath of covenant innocence, the covenant legal covenant presentation is complete. What follows — the Elihu speeches of Chapters 32-37 and YHWH's covenant speech from the whirlwind in Chapters 38-41 — responds to this covenant completed covenant case.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — did not he who made me in the womb make him? And did not one fashion us in the womb? Iyov's covenant most radical covenant theological statement about the covenant equality of servants and masters: the covenant servant and the covenant master share the same covenant Creator and the same covenant womb-origin. The covenant social covenant hierarchy cannot override the covenant creational covenant equality. This covenant ethical covenant insight is grounded in covenant creation covenant theology.

ELIHU — CHAPTERS 32 THROUGH 37 — THE FOURTH VOICE

IYOV — CHAPTER 32 These Three Men Ceased to Answer Iyov — Then Elihu the Son of Barachel the Buzite Burned With Anger — He Was Angry at Iyov — He Was Angry at His Three Friends — He Who Is Young in Years Ought to Speak

1 So these three men ceased to answer Iyov, because he was righteous in his own eyes.

2 Then Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, burned with anger. He burned with anger at Iyov because he justified himself rather than Creator.

3 He burned with anger also at Iyov's three friends because they had found no answer, although they had declared Iyov to be in the wrong.

4 Now Elihu had waited to speak to Iyov because they were older than he.

5 And when Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of these three men, he burned with anger.

6 And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said: I am young in years, and you are aged. Therefore I was timid and afraid to declare my opinion to you.

7 I said, Let days speak, and many years teach wisdom.

8 But it is the spirit in man, the breath of the Almighty, that makes him understand.

9 It is not the old who are wise, nor the aged who understand what is right.

10 Therefore I say, Listen to me. Let me also declare my opinion.

11 Behold, I waited for your words, I listened for your wise sayings, while you searched out what to say.

12 I gave you my attention, and, behold, there was none among you who refuted Iyov or who answered his words.

13 Beware of saying, We have found wisdom. Creator may vanquish him, not man.

14 He has not directed his words against me, and I will not answer him with your speeches.

15 They are dismayed. They answer no more. They have not a word to say.

16 And shall I wait, because they do not speak, because they stand there and answer no more?

17 I also will answer with my share. I also will declare my opinion.

18 For I am full of words. The spirit within me constrains me.

19 Behold, my belly is like wine that has no vent. Like new wineskins ready to burst.

20 I must speak, that I may find relief. I must open my lips and answer.

21 I will not show partiality to any man or use flattery toward any person.

22 For I do not know how to flatter, else my Maker would soon take me away.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. The Elihu section — Chapters 32-37 — is the most disputed section of the book's composition. Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite appears only here, is not mentioned in the prologue or epilogue, and addresses Iyov's specific arguments more directly than the three friends. The covenant speech of Elihu is the final covenant human covenant response before YHWH speaks — a covenant preparation that points toward the divine covenant answer.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 32 in full. Verse 8-9 — it is the spirit in man, the breath of the Almighty, that makes him understand. It is not the old who are wise, nor the aged who understand what is right. Elihu's covenant epistemological covenant correction of the covenant appeal to covenant tradition and age: covenant wisdom comes from the covenant breath of the Almighty — ruakh shaddai — not from covenant accumulated years. The covenant young Elihu challenges the covenant authority of the covenant aged friends.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 32 in full. Verse 12 — there was none among you who refuted Iyov or who answered his words. Elihu's covenant assessment of the three friends' covenant failure: they declared Iyov in the wrong but could not answer his covenant words. The covenant friends' covenant failure is precisely the failure that YHWH will confirm in Chapter 42:7 — they did not speak what was right about YHWH as his servant Iyov did.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-3 — Elihu burned with anger because Iyov justified himself rather than Creator — and because the three friends had found no answer, although they had declared Iyov to be in the wrong. Elihu identifies the double failure: Iyov has made his covenant own righteousness the primary reference point rather than YHWH's, and the friends have condemned Iyov without covenant adequate covenant argument. Elihu's covenant anger is directed at both parties — a covenant structural indicator that his covenant position will be different from both.

IYOV — CHAPTER 33 But Now Hear My Speech — I Am Toward Creator as You Are — In a Dream in a Vision of the Night — He Opens the Ears of Men — If There Is an Angel a Mediator — He Has Redeemed My Soul From Going Down Into the Pit

1 But now, hear my speech, O Iyov, and listen to all my words.

2 Behold, I open my mouth. The tongue in my mouth speaks.

3 My words declare the uprightness of my heart, and what my lips know they speak sincerely.

4 The Spirit of Creator has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.

5 Answer me, if you can. Set your words in order before me. Take your stand.

6 Behold, I am toward Creator as you are. I too was pinched off from a piece of clay.

7 Behold, no fear of me need terrify you. My pressure will not be heavy upon you.

8 Surely you have spoken in my ears, and I have heard the sound of your words.

9 You say, I am pure, without transgression. I am clean, and there is no iniquity in me.

10 Behold, he finds occasions against me. He counts me as his enemy.

11 He puts my feet in the stocks and watches all my paths.

12 Behold, in this you are not right. I will answer you, for Creator is greater than man.

13 Why do you contend against him, saying, He will answer none of man's words?

14 For Creator speaks in one way, and in two, though man does not perceive it.

15 In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falls on men, while they slumber on their beds,

16 Then he opens the ears of men and terrifies them with warnings,

17 That he may turn man aside from his deed and conceal pride from a man.

18 He keeps back his soul from the pit, his life from perishing by the sword.

19 Man is also rebuked with pain on his bed and with continual strife in his bones,

20 So that his life loathes bread, and his appetite the choicest food.

21 His flesh is so wasted away that it cannot be seen, and his bones that were not seen stick out.

22 His soul draws near the pit, and his life to those who bring death.

23 If there is for him an angel, a mediator, one of the thousand, to declare to man what is right for him,

24 And he is merciful to him, and says, Deliver him from going down into the pit. I have found a ransom.

25 Let his flesh become fresh with youth. Let him return to the days of his youthful vigor.

26 Then man prays to Creator, and he accepts him. He sees his face with a shout of joy, and he restores to man his righteousness.

27 He sings before men and says, I sinned and perverted what was right, and it was not repaid to me.

28 He has redeemed my soul from going down into the pit, and my life shall look upon the light.

29 Behold, Creator does all these things, twice, three times, with a man,

30 To bring back his soul from the pit, that he may be lighted with the light of life.

31 Pay attention, O Iyov, listen to me. Be silent, and I will speak.

32 If you have any words, answer me. Speak, for I desire to justify you.

33 If not, listen to me. Be silent, and I will teach you wisdom.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Elihu's first speech addresses Iyov's covenant claim that YHWH does not answer — Chapter 33:13. Elihu argues that YHWH does speak — in dreams and visions — verse 15-16 — and through physical suffering — verse 19-22 — as covenant corrective covenant communication. Verse 23-28 introduces the covenant mediating angel who declares to man what is right, finds a ransom, and delivers the man from the pit. This is the most developed covenant mediator theology in the covenant Iyov text.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 33 in full. Verse 23-24 — if there is for him an angel, a mediator, one of the thousand, to declare to man what is right for him, and he is merciful to him, and says, Deliver him from going down into the pit. I have found a ransom. The covenant intercessory angel who finds a covenant ransom and delivers the covenant man from the covenant pit is the most detailed covenant mediatorial covenant image in the covenant dialogue — anticipating the covenant resolution through covenant divine covenant initiative.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 33 in full. Verse 6-7 — behold, I am toward Creator as you are. I too was pinched off from a piece of clay. Behold, no fear of me need terrify you. Elihu's covenant offer of covenant equal-ground covenant dialogue: unlike YHWH whose covenant majesty terrifies — Chapter 9:34 — Elihu speaks from the same covenant clay as Iyov. The covenant offer is genuine and the covenant conversation is covenant safer than the covenant direct covenant encounter with YHWH.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — in this you are not right. I will answer you, for Creator is greater than man. Elihu acknowledges Iyov's specific covenant arguments — verses 9-11 quote Iyov's covenant claims — and engages them directly. Where the three friends ignored Iyov's specific covenant arguments and repeated their covenant system, Elihu cites and responds to Iyov's actual covenant words. This covenant methodological covenant difference is significant even if Elihu's covenant answer is also incomplete.

IYOV — CHAPTER 34 Hear My Words You Wise Men — Iyov Says I Am in the Right — Far Be It From Creator to Do Evil — He Pays Man According to His Work — He Is Never Unjust — The Godless Should Not Reign — Iyov Speaks Without Knowledge

1 Then Elihu answered and said:

2 Hear my words, you wise men, and give ear to me, you who know.

3 For the ear tests words as the palate tastes food.

4 Let us choose what is right. Let us know among ourselves what is good.

5 For Iyov has said, I am in the right, and Creator has taken away my right.

6 In spite of my right I am counted a liar. My wound is incurable, though I am without transgression.

7 What man is like Iyov, who drinks up scoffing like water,

8 Who travels in company with evildoers and walks with wicked men?

9 For he has said, It profits a man nothing that he should take delight in Creator.

10 Therefore, hear me, you men of understanding: far be it from Creator to do evil, and from the Almighty to do wrong.

11 For according to the work of a man he will repay him, and according to his ways he will make it befall him.

12 Of a truth, Creator will not do wickedly, and the Almighty will not pervert justice.

13 Who gave him charge over the earth, and who laid on him the whole world?

14 If he should set his heart to it and gather to himself his spirit and his breath,

15 All flesh would perish together, and man would return to dust.

16 If you have understanding, hear this. Listen to what I say.

17 Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn him who is righteous and mighty,

18 Who says to a king, Worthless one, and to nobles, Wicked man,

19 Who shows no partiality to princes, nor regards the rich more than the poor, for they are all the work of his hands?

20 In a moment they die. At midnight the people are shaken and pass away, and the mighty are taken away by no human hand.

21 For his eyes are on the ways of a man, and he sees all his steps.

22 There is no gloom or deep darkness where evildoers may hide themselves.

23 For Creator has no need to consider a man further, that he should go before Creator in judgment.

24 He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place.

25 Thus, knowing their works, he overturns them in the night, and they are crushed.

26 He strikes them for their wickedness in a place where others can see,

27 Because they turned aside from following him and had no regard for any of his ways,

28 So that they caused the cry of the poor to come to him, and he heard the cry of the afflicted.

29 When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can behold him, whether it be a nation or a man?

30 That a godless man should not reign, that he should not be a snare to the people.

31 For has anyone said to Creator, I have borne punishment. I will not offend anymore.

32 Teach me what I do not see. If I have done iniquity, I will do it no more.

33 Will he then make repayment to suit you, because you reject it? For you must choose, and not I. Therefore speak what you know.

34 Men of understanding will say to me, and the wise man who hears me will say:

35 Iyov speaks without knowledge, and his words are without insight.

36 Would that Iyov were tried to the end, because he answers like wicked men.

37 For he adds rebellion to his sin. He claps his hands among us and multiplies his words against Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Elihu's second speech defends YHWH's covenant justice more aggressively than the first: far be it from Creator to do evil — verse 10. The covenant defense of covenant divine covenant justice is legitimate; the covenant problem is Elihu's covenant specific application to Iyov: he claims Iyov has said it profits nothing to delight in Creator — verse 9 — which misquotes Iyov by attributing the words of the covenant wicked in Chapter 21:15 to Iyov himself.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 34 in full. Verse 10-12 — far be it from Creator to do evil, and from the Almighty to do wrong. For according to the work of a man he will repay him. Of a truth, Creator will not do wickedly, and the Almighty will not pervert justice. The covenant theological position is correct: Creator does not do evil. The covenant application to Iyov's covenant case — that his suffering must therefore be deserved — is the covenant same logical error the three friends made.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 34 in full. Verse 35-37 — Iyov speaks without knowledge, and his words are without insight. Would that Iyov were tried to the end, because he answers like wicked men. For he adds rebellion to his sin. Elihu's covenant condemnation of Iyov is harsher than anything the three friends said. YHWH's verdict in Chapter 42:7 — that Iyov spoke what was right — is the covenant direct covenant refutation of Elihu's covenant assessment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-6 — for Iyov has said, I am in the right, and Creator has taken away my right. In spite of my right I am counted a liar. Elihu accurately quotes Iyov's covenant position. The covenant difference between Elihu and the three friends is that Elihu engages with Iyov's covenant actual covenant words rather than ignoring them. But Elihu's covenant response — that YHWH does not pervert justice, therefore Iyov must be wrong — is the covenant same covenant logical structure as the friends': covenant premises correct, covenant application to Iyov's covenant case invalid.

IYOV — CHAPTER 35 Do You Think This Is Just — What Do You Give Creator — Your Wickedness Concerns a Man Like Yourself — Because of the Pride of Evil Men Creator Does Not Hear — Iyov Opens His Mouth in Emptiness

1 And Elihu answered and said:

2 Do you think this is just? Do you say, It is my right before Creator,

3 That you ask, What advantage have I? How am I better off than if I had sinned?

4 I will answer you and your friends with you.

5 Look at the heavens, and see. Observe the clouds, which are higher than you.

6 If you have sinned, what do you accomplish against him? And if your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to him?

7 If you are righteous, what do you give to him? Or what does he receive from your hand?

8 Your wickedness concerns a man like yourself, and your righteousness a son of man.

9 Because of the multitude of oppressions people cry out. They call for help because of the arm of the mighty.

10 But none says, Where is Creator my Maker, who gives songs in the night,

11 Who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the heavens?

12 There they cry out, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil men.

13 Surely Creator does not hear an empty cry, nor does the Almighty regard it.

14 How much less when you say that you do not see him, that the case is before him, and you are waiting for him.

15 And now, because his anger does not punish, and he does not take much note of transgression,

16 Iyov opens his mouth in emptiness. He multiplies words without knowledge.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Elihu's third speech is the briefest — sixteen verses — and addresses Iyov's claim that it profits nothing to delight in Creator. Elihu's response: human righteousness or wickedness does not affect Creator — verse 6-8. This is the same theological point Elifaz made in Chapter 22:2-4, and it is equally problematic: if human covenant behavior truly makes no difference to YHWH, the covenant theological foundation for covenant moral covenant accountability collapses.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 35 in full. Verse 10-11 — but none says, Where is Creator my Maker, who gives songs in the night, who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth? Elihu's covenant insight about the covenant community's covenant failure: people cry out under oppression but do not ask for Creator. The covenant cry for relief without the covenant seeking of Creator produces no covenant answer. The covenant distinction between covenant complaint and covenant prayer is the covenant point.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 35 in full. Verse 16 — Iyov opens his mouth in emptiness. He multiplies words without knowledge. Elihu's covenant final dismissal of Iyov's covenant argument. YHWH's covenant verdict in Chapter 42:7 — Iyov spoke what was right — is the covenant direct covenant answer to this covenant assessment. The covenant man whom Elihu calls covenant empty-mouthed is the covenant man YHWH will vindicate.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6-8 — if you have sinned, what do you accomplish against him? If your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to him? If you are righteous, what do you give to him? The covenant theological problem of divine impassibility: Elihu argues that human behavior makes no difference to the infinite Creator — and this covenant argument, while containing a covenant genuine covenant theological insight about YHWH's transcendence, simultaneously undermines the covenant moral seriousness of the covenant relationship.

IYOV — CHAPTER 36 Elihu Continued — Creator Is Mighty and Does Not Despise Any — He Delivers the Afflicted — He Opens Their Ears by Adversity — He Who Is Godless in Heart Stores Up Anger — Behold Creator Is Exalted in His Power

1 And Elihu continued, and said:

2 Bear with me a little, and I will show you, for I have yet something to say on Creator's behalf.

3 I will get my knowledge from afar and ascribe righteousness to my Maker.

4 For truly my words are not false. One who is perfect in knowledge is with you.

5 Behold, Creator is mighty, and does not despise any. He is mighty in strength of understanding.

6 He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives the afflicted their right.

7 He does not withdraw his eyes from the righteous, but with kings on the throne he sets them forever, and they are exalted.

8 And if they are bound in chains and caught in the cords of affliction,

9 Then he declares to them their work and their transgressions, that they are behaving arrogantly.

10 He opens their ears to instruction and commands that they return from iniquity.

11 If they listen and serve him, they complete their days in prosperity, and their years in pleasantness.

12 But if they do not listen, they perish by the sword and die without knowledge.

13 The godless in heart store up anger. They do not cry for help when he binds them.

14 They die in youth, and their life ends among the cult prostitutes.

15 He delivers the afflicted by their affliction and opens their ear by adversity.

16 He also allured you out of distress into a broad place where there was no cramping, and what was set on your table was full of fatness.

17 But you are full of the judgment on the wicked. Judgment and justice seize you.

18 Beware lest wrath entice you into scoffing, and let not the greatness of the ransom turn you aside.

19 Will your cry for help avail to keep you from distress, or all the force of your strength?

20 Do not long for the night, when peoples vanish in their place.

21 Take care. Do not turn to iniquity, for this you have chosen rather than affliction.

22 Behold, Creator is exalted in his power. Who is a teacher like him?

23 Who has prescribed for him his way, or who can say, You have done wrong?

24 Remember to extol his work, of which men have sung.

25 All mankind has looked on it. Man beholds it from afar.

26 Behold, Creator is great, and we know him not. The number of his years is unsearchable.

27 For he draws up the drops of water. They distill his mist in rain,

28 Which the skies pour down and drop on mankind abundantly.

29 Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds, the thunderings of his pavilion?

30 Behold, he scatters his lightning about him and covers the roots of the sea.

31 For by these he judges peoples. He gives food in abundance.

32 He covers his hands with the lightning and commands it to strike the mark.

33 Its crashing declares his presence. The cattle also declare that he rises.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Elihu's fourth speech begins a covenant magnificent covenant transition from the covenant dialogue to the divine covenant speech: he moves from covenant theological covenant argument to covenant covenant creation covenant description — rain, clouds, lightning — verses 27-33. The covenant creation speech of Elihu anticipates YHWH's covenant speech from the whirlwind in Chapters 38-39. Elihu serves as the covenant literary bridge between the covenant human covenant dialogue and the covenant divine covenant address.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 36 in full. Verse 15 — he delivers the afflicted by their affliction and opens their ear by adversity. Elihu's covenant most insightful statement: the covenant affliction itself is the covenant instrument of covenant divine covenant communication. The covenant suffering that Iyov experiences as covenant divine covenant hostility is, in Elihu's covenant framework, the covenant means by which YHWH opens the covenant ears of the covenant afflicted. The covenant insight is genuine; the covenant application to Iyov remains problematic.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 36 in full. Verse 26 — behold, Creator is great, and we know him not. The number of his years is unsearchable. Elihu's covenant acknowledgment of covenant divine covenant incomprehensibility: the same covenant theme that Iyov stated with greater covenant urgency in Chapter 9 and the covenant Wisdom Hymn stated in Chapter 28. The covenant book converges on the covenant acknowledgment that YHWH exceeds all covenant human covenant categories.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22 — behold, Creator is exalted in his power. Who is a teacher like him? The covenant rhetorical question that Elihu asks as covenant preparation for the covenant divine covenant speech: no one is a teacher like YHWH. The covenant implication is that YHWH's own covenant speech from the whirlwind — which is about to come — will be the covenant ultimate covenant instruction that no covenant human covenant speech, including Elihu's, can provide.

IYOV — CHAPTER 37 At This Also My Heart Trembles — Creator Thunders Wondrously With His Voice — He Seals Up the Hand of Every Man — Do You Know How Creator Lays His Command — Can You Like Him Spread Out the Skies — Stop and Consider the Wondrous Works of Creator

1 At this also my heart trembles and leaps out of its place.

2 Keep listening to the thunder of his voice and the rumbling that comes from his mouth.

3 Under the whole heaven he lets it go, and his lightning to the corners of the earth.

4 After it his voice roars. He thunders with his majestic voice, and he does not restrain the lightnings when his voice is heard.

5 Creator thunders wondrously with his voice. He does great things that we cannot comprehend.

6 For to the snow he says, Fall on the earth, likewise to the downpour, his mighty downpour.

7 He seals up the hand of every man, that all men whom he made may know it.

8 Then the beasts go into their lairs, and remain in their dens.

9 From its chamber comes the whirlwind, and cold from the scattering winds.

10 By the breath of Creator ice is given, and the broad waters are frozen fast.

11 He loads the thick cloud with moisture. The clouds scatter his lightning.

12 They turn around and around by his guidance, to accomplish all that he commands them on the face of the habitable world.

13 Whether for correction or for his land or for love, he causes it to happen.

14 Hear this, O Iyov. Stop and consider the wondrous works of Creator.

15 Do you know how Creator lays his command upon them and causes the lightning of his cloud to shine?

16 Do you know the balancings of the clouds, the wondrous works of him who is perfect in knowledge,

17 You whose garments are hot when the earth is still because of the south wind?

18 Can you, like him, spread out the skies, hard as a cast metal mirror?

19 Teach us what we shall say to him. We cannot draw up our case because of darkness.

20 Shall it be told him that I would speak? Did a man ever wish that he would be swallowed up?

21 And now no one looks at the light when it is bright in the skies, when the wind has passed and cleared them.

22 Out of the north comes golden splendor. Creator is clothed with awesome majesty.

23 The Almighty — we cannot find him. He is great in power. Justice and abundant righteousness he will not violate.

24 Therefore men fear him. He does not regard any who are wise in their own conceit.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 37 is the climax of Elihu's covenant speeches — a covenant magnificent covenant description of covenant storm phenomena that transitions directly into YHWH's covenant speech from the whirlwind in Chapter 38. The covenant literary connection is precise: Elihu describes the storm — verse 9, from its chamber comes the whirlwind — and YHWH speaks from the storm. Elihu's covenant preparation for the covenant divine covenant encounter is both covenant literary and covenant theological.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 37 in full. Verse 14 — hear this, O Iyov. Stop and consider the wondrous works of Creator. Elihu's covenant final direct address to Iyov before YHWH speaks is the covenant invitation to the covenant posture that YHWH's covenant speech will require: stop, consider, wonder. The covenant posture of covenant contemplative covenant awe before the covenant wonders of creation is the covenant alternative to Iyov's covenant legal covenant confrontation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 37 in full. Verse 19 — teach us what we shall say to him. We cannot draw up our case because of darkness. Elihu's covenant acknowledgment of his own covenant limitation: he too cannot covenant draw up a covenant case before YHWH. The covenant acknowledgment serves as the covenant transition: no covenant human speech can adequately represent the covenant case before YHWH. Only YHWH's own covenant speech can address the covenant question that Iyov has raised.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 23-24 — the Almighty — we cannot find him. He is great in power. Justice and abundant righteousness he will not violate. Therefore men fear him. He does not regard any who are wise in their own conceit. Elihu's covenant final covenant statement is simultaneously the covenant transition to YHWH's covenant speech and the covenant inadvertent covenant self-indictment: the one who does not regard those wise in their own conceit — this covenant statement applies to the covenant friends and to Elihu himself.

YHWH SPEAKS FROM THE WHIRLWIND — CHAPTERS 38 THROUGH 42

IYOV — CHAPTER 38 Then YHWH Answered Iyov Out of the Whirlwind — Who Is This That Darkens Counsel — Where Were You When I Laid the Foundation of the Earth — Have You Entered Into the Springs of the Sea — Have You Comprehended the Expanse of the Earth — Have You Entered the Storehouses of the Snow

1 Then YHWH answered Iyov out of the whirlwind and said:

2 Who is this that darkens counsel by words without knowledge?

3 Dress for action like a man. I will question you, and you make it known to me.

4 Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding.

5 Who determined its measurements — surely you know. Or who stretched the line upon it?

6 On what were its bases sunk, or who laid its cornerstone,

7 When the morning stars sang together and all the sons of Creator shouted for joy?

8 Or who shut in the sea with doors when it burst out from the womb,

9 When I made clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling band,

10 And prescribed limits for it and set bars and doors,

11 And said, Thus far shall you come, and no farther, and here shall your proud waves be stayed?

12 Have you commanded the morning since your days began, and caused the dawn to know its place,

13 That it might take hold of the skirts of the earth, and the wicked be shaken out of it?

14 It is changed like clay under the seal, and its features stand out like a garment.

15 From the wicked their light is withheld, and their uplifted arm is broken.

16 Have you entered into the springs of the sea, or walked in the recesses of the deep?

17 Have the gates of death been revealed to you, or have you seen the gates of deep darkness?

18 Have you comprehended the expanse of the earth? Declare, if you know all this.

19 Where is the way to the dwelling of light, and where is the place of darkness,

20 That you may take it to its territory and that you may discern the paths to its home?

21 You know, for you were born then, and the number of your days is great.

22 Have you entered the storehouses of the snow, or have you seen the storehouses of the hail,

23 Which I have reserved for the time of trouble, for the day of battle and war?

24 What is the way to the place where the light is distributed, or where the east wind is scattered upon the earth?

25 Who has cleft a channel for the torrents of rain and a way for the thunderbolt,

26 To bring rain on a land where no man is, on the desert in which there is no man,

27 To satisfy the waste and desolate land, and to make the ground sprout with grass?

28 Has the rain a father, or who has begotten the drops of dew?

29 From whose womb did the ice come forth, and who has given birth to the frost of heaven?

30 The waters become hard like stone, and the face of the deep is frozen.

31 Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades or loose the belt of Orion?

32 Can you lead forth the Mazzaroth in their season, or can you guide the Bear with its children?

33 Do you know the ordinances of the heavens? Can you establish their rule on the earth?

34 Can you lift up your voice to the clouds, that a flood of waters may cover you?

35 Can you send forth lightnings, that they may go and say to you, Here we are?

36 Who has put wisdom in the inward parts or given understanding to the mind?

37 Who can number the clouds by wisdom? Or who can tilt the waterskins of the heavens,

38 When the dust runs into a mass and the clods stick together?

39 Can you hunt the prey for the lion, or satisfy the appetite of the young lions,

40 When they crouch in their dens or lie in wait in their thicket?

41 Who provides for the raven its prey, when its young ones cry to Creator for help, and wander about for lack of food?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 38 is the beginning of YHWH's speech from the whirlwind — the most magnificent covenant theophany in the covenant wisdom literature. YHWH does not answer Iyov's covenant specific covenant questions. Instead, YHWH answers the covenant deeper covenant question underneath all of Iyov's covenant questions: who has the covenant knowledge required to understand and to judge the covenant total governance of the covenant universe? The answer is the answer to every covenant human covenant claim to understand YHWH's covenant ways.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 38 in full. Verse 7 — when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of Creator shouted for joy. The covenant creation event as covenant communal covenant celebration: the laying of the foundation of the earth was accompanied by the covenant singing of the covenant morning stars and the covenant joy-shout of the covenant sons of Creator. The covenant creation is the covenant occasion of the covenant first covenant joy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 38 in full. Verse 3 — dress for action like a man. I will question you, and you make it known to me. YHWH's covenant invitation to Iyov is not covenant dismissal but covenant engagement: dress for action — gird your loins — is the covenant posture of covenant readiness for covenant serious covenant encounter. YHWH treats Iyov's covenant challenge as covenant worthy of covenant direct covenant response.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — who is this that darkens counsel by words without knowledge? The covenant irony: YHWH does not accuse Iyov of covenant sin — which the friends did repeatedly. YHWH accuses Iyov of speaking without covenant knowledge. The covenant accusation of covenant ignorance rather than covenant moral covenant failure is itself the covenant partial vindication of Iyov's covenant claim of covenant innocence: the covenant problem was not covenant wickedness but covenant insufficient covenant knowledge of the covenant ways of YHWH.

IYOV — CHAPTER 39 Do You Know When the Mountain Goats Give Birth — Who Has Let the Wild Donkey Go Free — Is the Wild Ox Willing to Serve You — The Wing of the Ostrich Waves Proudly — Do You Give the Horse His Might — Does the Hawk Soar by Your Wisdom — Does the Eagle Mount Up at Your Command

1 Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Do you observe the calving of the does?

2 Can you number the months that they fulfill, and do you know the time when they give birth,

3 When they crouch, bring forth their offspring, and are delivered of their young?

4 Their young ones become strong. They grow up in the open. They go out and do not return to them.

5 Who has let the wild donkey go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey,

6 To whom I have given the arid plain for his home and the salt land for his dwelling place?

7 He scorns the tumult of the city. He hears not the shouts of the driver.

8 He ranges the mountains as his pasture, and he searches after every green thing.

9 Is the wild ox willing to serve you? Will he spend the night at your manger?

10 Can you bind him in the furrow with ropes, or will he harrow the valleys after you?

11 Will you depend on him because his strength is great, and will you leave to him your labor?

12 Do you have faith in him that he will return your grain and gather it to your threshing floor?

13 The wings of the ostrich wave proudly, but are they the pinions and plumage of love?

14 For she leaves her eggs to the earth and lets them be warmed on the ground,

15 Forgetting that a foot may crush them and that the wild beast may trample them.

16 She deals cruelly with her young, as if they were not hers. Though her labor be in vain, yet she has no fear,

17 Because Creator has made her forget wisdom and given her no share in understanding.

18 When she rouses herself to flee, she laughs at the horse and his rider.

19 Do you give the horse his might? Do you clothe his neck with a mane?

20 Do you make him leap like the locust? His majestic snorting is terrifying.

21 He paws in the valley and exults in his strength. He goes out to meet the weapons.

22 He laughs at fear and is not dismayed. He does not turn back from the sword.

23 Upon him rattle the quiver, the flashing spear, and the javelin.

24 With fierceness and rage he swallows the ground. He cannot stand still at the sound of the trumpet.

25 When the trumpet sounds, he says, Aha! He smells the battle from afar, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.

26 Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads his wings toward the south?

27 Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes his nest on high?

28 On the rock he dwells and makes his home, on the rocky crag and stronghold.

29 From there he spies out the prey. His eyes behold it from far away.

30 His young ones suck up blood, and where the slain are, there is he.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 39 continues YHWH's covenant speech with a covenant gallery of covenant wild covenant creatures: mountain goats, wild donkey, wild ox, ostrich, warhorse, hawk, eagle. YHWH's covenant point is covenant the same as in Chapter 38 but from the covenant perspective of covenant living covenant creatures: each creature operates according to its own covenant created covenant nature with covenant wisdom given by YHWH. No covenant human being controls or comprehends this covenant vast covenant ecosystem of covenant created freedom.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 39 in full. Verse 19-25 — the warhorse passage is the most celebrated covenant nature poem in the covenant Bible: the horse that laughs at fear, does not turn back from the sword, cannot stand still at the sound of the trumpet, smells the battle from afar. The covenant creature's covenant magnificent covenant freedom within its covenant created covenant nature is the covenant image of the covenant creation order that operates entirely outside covenant human covenant understanding or covenant control.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 39 in full. Verse 5-8 — who has let the wild donkey go free? To whom I have given the arid plain for his home. The wild donkey scorns the tumult of the city and hears not the shouts of the driver. YHWH's covenant gift of covenant freedom to the covenant wild donkey: the covenant creature that in human hands becomes a covenant beast of burden is in YHWH's covenant creation order a covenant free covenant wanderer of the covenant arid plain. The covenant covenant creation order cannot be reduced to covenant human covenant utility.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — because Creator has made her forget wisdom and given her no share in understanding. The covenant ostrich passage is theologically significant: YHWH explicitly acknowledges that the ostrich was made without wisdom. The covenant creation order contains creatures that operate without covenant conventional covenant wisdom — and they flourish. The covenant assumption that covenant wisdom is uniformly distributed across covenant creation is challenged by the covenant example of the covenant triumphant ostrich.

IYOV — CHAPTER 40 And YHWH Said to Iyov — Shall a Faultfinder Contend With the Almighty — Iyov Answered and Said I Am of Small Account — Gird Up Your Loins Like a Man — Have You an Arm Like Creator — Behold Behemot Which I Made as I Made You

1 And YHWH said to Iyov:

2 Shall a faultfinder contend with the Almighty? He who argues with Creator, let him answer it.

3 Then Iyov answered YHWH and said:

4 Behold, I am of small account. What shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth.

5 I have spoken once, and I will not answer. Twice, but I will proceed no further.

6 Then YHWH answered Iyov out of the whirlwind and said:

7 Dress for action like a man. I will question you, and you make it known to me.

8 Will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be in the right?

9 Have you an arm like Creator, and can you thunder with a voice like his?

10 Adorn yourself with majesty and dignity. Clothe yourself with glory and splendor.

11 Pour out the overflowings of your anger, and look on everyone who is proud and abase him.

12 Look on everyone who is proud and bring him low and tread down the wicked where they stand.

13 Hide them all in the dust together. Bind their faces in the world below.

14 Then will I also acknowledge to you that your own right hand can save you.

15 Behold, Behemot, which I made as I made you. He eats grass like an ox.

16 Behold, his strength in his loins, and his power in the muscles of his belly.

17 He makes his tail stiff like a cedar. The sinews of his thighs are knit together.

18 His bones are tubes of bronze. His limbs are like bars of iron.

19 He is the first of the works of Creator. Let him who made him bring near his sword.

20 For the mountains yield food for him where all the wild beasts play.

21 Under the lotus plants he lies, in the shelter of the reeds and in the marsh.

22 For his shade the lotus trees cover him. The willows of the brook surround him.

23 Behold, if the river is turbulent he is not frightened. He is confident though Yarden rushes against his mouth.

24 Can one take him by his eyes, or pierce his nose with a snare?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 40 introduces Behemot — the covenant great land creature — as YHWH's covenant example of covenant creation that exceeds covenant human covenant control. YHWH's challenge to Iyov — verse 8-14 — is the covenant most direct covenant statement in the entire covenant speech: if Iyov can humble the covenant proud and tread down the covenant wicked, then YHWH will acknowledge that Iyov's own right hand can save him. The covenant challenge reveals that the covenant inability to govern the covenant creation order also implies the covenant inability to govern the covenant moral order.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 40 in full. Verse 4-5 — behold, I am of small account. What shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth. I have spoken once, and I will not answer. Twice, but I will proceed no further. Iyov's first response to YHWH's speech is the covenant posture of covenant profound covenant humility: the covenant man who declared his covenant case with covenant magnificent covenant confidence now places his hand on his mouth. The covenant encounter with YHWH produces covenant genuine covenant reconsideration.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 40 in full. Verse 15 — behold, Behemot, which I made as I made you. The covenant most important phrase in the covenant Behemot description: Behemot was made as Iyov was made — both are the covenant work of the same covenant Creator. The covenant man and the covenant great beast share a covenant common covenant origin in YHWH's covenant creative covenant act. The covenant creation order that includes Behemot also includes Iyov — both are sustained by the same covenant care.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be in the right? YHWH's covenant direct address of the covenant theological structure of Iyov's covenant argument: Iyov's covenant claim of covenant innocence requires that YHWH be in the wrong. YHWH acknowledges the covenant tension without dismissing Iyov's covenant experience. The covenant question is not a covenant denial of Iyov's suffering but a covenant invitation to see the covenant total covenant picture that Iyov's covenant limited covenant perspective cannot accommodate.

IYOV — CHAPTER 41 Can You Draw Out Leviathan With a Hook — Will He Make a Covenant With You — No One Is So Fierce That He Dares to Stir Him Up — Who Then Is He Who Can Stand Before Me — Out of His Mouth Go Flaming Torches — He Is King Over All the Sons of Pride

1 Can you draw out Leviathan with a hook, or press down his tongue with a cord?

2 Can you put a rope in his nose or pierce his jaw with a hook?

3 Will he make many pleas to you? Will he speak to you soft words?

4 Will he make a covenant with you to take him for your servant forever?

5 Will you play with him as with a bird, or will you put him on a leash for your girls?

6 Will traders bargain over him? Will they divide him up among the merchants?

7 Can you fill his skin with harpoons or his head with fishing spears?

8 Lay your hands on him. Remember the battle — you will not do it again.

9 Behold, the hope of a man is false. He is laid low even at the sight of him.

10 No one is so fierce that he dares to stir him up. Who then is he who can stand before me?

11 Who has first given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine.

12 I will not keep silence concerning his limbs, or his mighty strength, or his goodly frame.

13 Who can strip off his outer garment? Who would come near him with a bridle?

14 Who can open the doors of his face? Around his teeth is terror.

15 His back is made of rows of shields, shut up closely as with a seal.

16 One is so near to another that no air can come between them.

17 They are joined one to another. They clasp each other and cannot be separated.

18 His sneezings flash forth light, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the dawn.

19 Out of his mouth go flaming torches. Sparks of fire leap forth.

20 Out of his nostrils comes forth smoke, as from a boiling pot and burning rushes.

21 His breath sets coals ablaze, and a flame comes forth from his mouth.

22 In his neck abides strength, and terror dances before him.

23 The folds of his flesh stick together, firmly cast on him and immovable.

24 His heart is hard as a stone, hard as the lower millstone.

25 When he raises himself up, the mighty are afraid. At the crashing they are beside themselves.

26 Though the sword reaches him, it does not avail, nor the spear, the dart, or the javelin.

27 He counts iron as straw, and bronze as rotten wood.

28 The arrow cannot make him flee. Sling stones are turned to stubble for him.

29 Clubs are counted as stubble. He laughs at the rattle of javelins.

30 His underparts are like sharp potsherds. He spreads himself like a threshing sledge on the mire.

31 He makes the deep boil like a pot. He makes the sea like a pot of ointment.

32 Behind him he leaves a shining wake. One would think the deep to be white-haired.

33 On earth there is not his like, a creature without fear.

34 He sees everything that is high. He is king over all the sons of pride.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text of Chapter 41. The Leviathan speech — which parallels the Behemot speech in Chapter 40 — brings YHWH's covenant creation discourse to its covenant climax with the covenant most covenant magnificent and covenant most covenant untameable of all covenant creatures. Verse 10-11 contains the covenant theological pivot: no one is so fierce that he dares to stir up Leviathan — who then is he who can stand before me? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 41 in full. Verse 34 — he sees everything that is high. He is king over all the sons of pride. Leviathan's covenant position as covenant king over the covenant proud is the covenant final covenant image of YHWH's covenant creation speech: the covenant most terrifying creature in the covenant creation order is the covenant subject and the covenant instrument of YHWH's covenant sovereign covenant governance. Even covenant primordial covenant chaos serves YHWH's covenant purposes.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 41 in full. Verse 11 — who has first given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine. YHWH's covenant response to Iyov's covenant implicit covenant claim that YHWH owes him something: there is no covenant prior covenant gift to YHWH that obligates YHWH to repay anyone. The covenant total covenant ownership of everything under heaven is the covenant foundation of covenant absolute covenant sovereignty.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — no one is so fierce that he dares to stir him up. Who then is he who can stand before me? The covenant logic of the entire speech: if covenant human beings cannot control Leviathan — the covenant most powerful covenant creature — how can they presume to call YHWH to covenant account for the covenant governance of the covenant entire creation order? The covenant argument is not the covenant silencing of Iyov's covenant complaint but the covenant reorientation of his covenant perspective.

IYOV — CHAPTER 42 Then Iyov Answered YHWH — I Had Heard You With My Ears But Now My Eye Sees You — My Anger Burns Against You and Your Two Friends — My Servant Iyov Will Pray for You — YHWH Restored the Fortunes of Iyov — And YHWH Blessed the Latter Days of Iyov More Than His Beginning

1 Then Iyov answered YHWH and said:

2 I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.

3 Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.

4 Hear, and I will speak. I will question you, and you make it known to me.

5 I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you.

6 Therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes.

7 After YHWH had spoken these words to Iyov, YHWH said to Elifaz the Temanite, My anger burns against you and against your two friends, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Iyov has.

8 Now therefore take seven bulls and seven rams and go to my servant Iyov and offer up a burnt offering for yourselves. And my servant Iyov shall pray for you, for I will accept his prayer not to deal with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Iyov has.

9 So Elifaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Tzofar the Na'amathite went and did what YHWH had told them, and YHWH accepted Iyov's prayer.

10 And YHWH restored the fortunes of Iyov, when he had prayed for his friends. And YHWH gave Iyov twice as much as he had before.

11 Then came to him all his brothers and sisters and all who had known him before, and ate bread with him in his house. And they showed him sympathy and comforted him for all the evil that YHWH had brought upon him. And each of them gave him a piece of money and a ring of gold.

12 And YHWH blessed the latter days of Iyov more than his beginning. And he had 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 yoke of oxen, and 1,000 female donkeys.

13 He had also seven sons and three daughters.

14 And he called the name of the first daughter Yemimah, and the name of the second Ketziah, and the name of the third Keren-Happukh.

15 And in all the land there were no women so beautiful as Iyov's daughters. And their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.

16 And after this Iyov lived 140 years, and saw his sons, and his sons' sons, four generations.

17 And Iyov died, an old man, and full of days.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text of Chapter 42. The covenant epilogue vindicates everything the covenant prologue established and everything Iyov maintained through the covenant dialogue: YHWH declares that Iyov spoke what was right — verse 7-8 — and restores Iyov twice over — verse 10-12. The covenant friends who spoke covenant systematic covenant theology are rebuked. The covenant man who spoke covenant honest covenant protest from covenant authentic covenant suffering is vindicated.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 42 in full. Verse 5 — I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you. The covenant transformation of Iyov through the covenant encounter with YHWH: the covenant hearing — covenant secondhand covenant knowledge of YHWH through covenant tradition and covenant teaching — has been replaced by covenant direct covenant vision. The covenant suffering has produced not covenant defeat but covenant deeper covenant knowledge of Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 42 in full. Verse 7 — my anger burns against you and against your two friends, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Iyov has. YHWH's covenant verdict is the covenant resolution of the entire covenant book: Iyov — who argued with YHWH, protested YHWH's covenant treatment, demanded an explanation — spoke what was right. The friends — who defended YHWH's covenant justice with systematic covenant theological arguments — did not speak what was right.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — and YHWH restored the fortunes of Iyov, when he had prayed for his friends. The covenant restoration comes through covenant prayer for the covenant friends who wronged him: Iyov's covenant restoration is simultaneous with his covenant intercession for those who compounded his covenant suffering with their covenant false covenant accusations. The covenant man who maintained covenant integrity through covenant suffering demonstrates covenant integrity through covenant forgiveness. The covenant book ends with the covenant covenant of covenant community restored through covenant intercession.

THE BOOK OF IYOV — COMPLETE

Volume Twenty-Five — The Ancient Covenant Record
